# Decentralized Wastewater Treatment

## Advances in technology have made decentralized wastewater treatment and water reuse viable even in areas off the grid

In an age of water scarcity, it's necessary to make the most of all water sources, whether that means treating sewage to prevent the pollution of surface water, or treating it further for safe reuse in irrigation or even for drinking.

Fluence's decentralized wastewater solutions take treatment to the point of need and are efficient enough to be deployed even in remote areas off the grid. Decentralized wastewater treatment is ideal for a variety of residential, commercial, and industrial settings, including:

In fact, Fluence decentralized wastewater plants have been playing a role in China's five-year plan, which includes bringing sewage treatment to small villages and remediating polluted rivers.

### Best-in-Class Decentralized Wastewater Treatment Technologies

Fluence has a range of efficient, plug-and-play wastewater treatment and reuse solutions. Our products are pre-engineered and preassembled, and can be up and running in record time. They're neighborhood-friendly, producing minimal noise and odor. And they're flexible: Treatment can be scaled up by operating two or more units in tandem, and when requirements change, they can easily be redeployed or sold.

##### Decentralized Wastewater Treatment with MABR

Learn more about Fluence’s [MABR technology](https://www.fluencecorp.com/mabr/).

##### Aspiral™ Smart Containerized Wastewater Solutions

Fluence’s Aspiral is a decentralized wastewater treatment solution that packages MABR technology in standard-sized shipping containers. Its small- to medium-sized plants are ideal for serving small towns, villages, residential communities, resorts, and more. Aspiral's efficiency makes it suitable for decentralized wastewater treatment off the grid since its low energy needs can be supplied by alternative sources.

The Aspiral Family offers several model sizes and configurations, with treatment capacities starting at 20 m³/d and going up to 340 m³/d with multiple units running in tandem. For operating ease, the systems can be remotely monitored and controlled. Tertiary treatment such as filtration and disinfection are compatible with the process and can be provided separately.

Learn more about Fluence’s [Aspiral™ Smart Containerized MABR plants](https://www.fluencecorp.com/aspiral/).

### Decentralized Wastewater Treatment Benefits

**Economy and Efficiency**  
Fluence decentralized wastewater treatment plants are housed in standard-sized shipping containers and are ready to go. They're engineered for fast deployment, as well as simple operation and maintenance.

**Flexibility and Scalability**  
Due to smart pre-engineering and design, Aspiral™ units are ready for integration with other systems and equipment. They are completely modular, allowing for capacity upgrades if required. Multiple pre-engineered configurations are available, enabling fast delivery, integration, commissioning, and operation.

**Sustainability**  
Low energy consumption and chemical use reduce the environmental impact of our decentralized wastewater treatment solutions. Additionally, Aspiral's unique design slashes the energy required for aeration by up to 90%.

**Compactness and Portability**  
The compact footprint of Fluence decentralized wastewater solutions minimizes site impact, lowers the cost of site development, and ensures easy expansion with minimum disruption to neighbors or the environment. The modular containers can be easily moved for redeployment or resale should needs change

**Operational Intelligence**  
Operating systems are fully automated, and remotely monitored and operated, which allows for the optimization of processes. Our Smart Containerized designs keep ongoing equipment, operation, and maintenance expenses in check.

### What Is Decentralized Wastewater Treatment?

Decentralized wastewater treatment, also known as distributed wastewater treatment, is the practice of locating wastewater treatment plants at the site of supply, demand, or ideally both. It’s a flexible, sustainable alternative to large wastewater treatment facilities that require expensive supply and delivery infrastructure.

#### Aspiral™ Decentralized Wastewater Treatment Plant in Rural China

With a Smart Containerized Aspiral™ system from Fluence, a remote Chinese village of 5,000 people has gone from having no wastewater treatment at all to having an effective, economical system. The 300 m³/d plant, which was installed and commissioned in just 10 days, produces an effluent that exceeds China's strict Class 1A standard.

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20800%20400'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![](https://www.fluencecorp.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/07/hubei-aspiral.jpg?_t=1586817255)

#### Smart Containerized Aspiral™ MABR for Highway Service Area

In China, a Fluence Aspiral system was chosen to provide a highway service area with decentralized, environmentally friendly wastewater treatment. Because the area includes restaurants, accommodations, and shopping, the plant had to be aesthetically pleasing and environmentally friendly, with minimal noise and odor. The 200 m³/d plant was commissioned within two months.

![](https://globalwaterforum.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/03/LOGO-PLUS-TEXT-100X500-300x60.png)

# Decentralized Water Treatment Solutions: Empowering Communities With Localized Systems

January 16th, 2025

![Share](https://static.addtoany.com/buttons/share_save_120_16.png)
![Print Friendly, PDF & Email](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAIAAAAAAAP///yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7)![Print Friendly, PDF & Email](https://cdn.printfriendly.com/buttons/printfriendly-pdf-email-button-notext.png)

![Print Friendly, PDF & Email](https://cdn.printfriendly.com/buttons/printfriendly-pdf-email-button-notext.png)

**Ellie Gabel, Revolutionized, USA**

*Access to clean water is fundamental to health, economic development and environmental sustainability. Yet, while effective in many regions, centralized water treatment systems often fail to meet the needs of remote or underserved communities. Here, Ellie Gabel discusses why decentralized water treatment systems are emerging as a transformative solution, providing localized, adaptable and cost-effective ways to ensure safe drinking water for all.*

Decentralized water treatment refers to small-scale, [localized systems that treat water near](https://www.nowra.org/library/what-is-decentralized-wastewater-treatment/) the point of use. Unlike centralized systems, which rely on extensive infrastructure to transport and distribute water over long distances, decentralized systems focus on addressing local water quality challenges.

These systems can range from household-level filters to community-based treatment plants, often utilizing advanced technologies such as reverse osmosis, ultraviolet (UV) disinfection and biological treatment processes.

**Why Decentralized Solutions Matter**

Centralized water systems have long been the backbone of urban and regional water management, but they aren’t without limitations. In many cases, these systems fail to address the unique challenges remote, underserved or disaster-stricken communities face.

Decentralized water treatment systems offer a promising alternative, addressing these gaps through localized, adaptable and efficient approaches. Here are some key reasons why decentralized systems are essential:

***Accessibility for Remote Areas***

Centralized systems can be costly to install and maintain for rural or geographically challenging communities.

Decentralized systems offer a practical alternative, reducing the need for extensive pipeline networks and enabling water treatment close to the source. They effectively serve small and dispersed populations by minimizing infrastructure costs and providing flexibility in design and implementation.

***Cost-Effectiveness***

Centralized water treatment systems require significant infrastructure, maintenance and energy investments.

Decentralized systems, on the other hand, can be scaled to meet specific local needs, lowering initial costs and operational expenses. They can be particularly advantageous in addressing non-potable water needs — such as irrigation or industrial use — without overburdening centralized facilities.

***Resilience in Crisis Situations***

Natural disasters, aging infrastructure and water contamination events highlight the vulnerability of centralized systems. Decentralized water treatment can provide resilience by diversifying water sources and offering redundant systems that ensure continuity of service during emergencies.

**How Decentralized Systems Work**

The effectiveness of decentralized water treatment lies in its versatility and innovative design. These systems are not a one-size-fits-all solution — instead, they employ various technologies and strategies to address local water quality issues.

From modular setups that can be scaled as needed to advanced treatment methods tailored to specific contaminants, decentralized systems are engineered for flexibility.

Industrial water systems [demand consistent water quality testing](https://www.aquaphoenixsci.com/how-to-analyze-your-water-testing-results/) to prevent contamination and ensure the reliability of decentralized treatment technologies in meeting safety standards. Regular testing safeguards health and helps fine-tune these systems, allowing them to adapt to changing water conditions and maintain peak efficiency.

Decentralized systems provide practical and resilient solutions by leveraging precise testing and adaptable designs, making them indispensable parts of modern water management strategies.

**Modular Design**

Decentralized water treatment systems are often modular, allowing for easy expansion or reconfiguration. This adaptability makes them suitable for growing communities or areas with fluctuating water demands.

One widely used approach is containerized systems, which house treatment equipment in mobile units. These systems can be transported and installed quickly, providing an immediate solution in disaster zones or temporary settlements.

**Diverse Treatment Technologies**

Decentralized systems employ various technologies tailored to local water quality issues. Common methods include:

These technologies’ adaptability ensures that systems can address a wide range of contaminants, from microbial pathogens to heavy metals.

In the United States, decentralized wastewater treatment systems are increasingly recognized as [integral components of rural wastewater](https://www.rd.usda.gov/newsroom/news-release/usda-and-epa-strengthen-partnership-improve-access-modern-and-affordable-wastewater-infrastructure) infrastructure. These systems prevent pollution, protect public health and reduce strain on aging centralized infrastructure.

While decentralized systems offer significant benefits, challenges remain. Maintenance and operation require technical expertise, which may be limited in some regions. Financing can also be a hurdle, particularly for small communities with constrained budgets.

**Future Potential**

Advancements in technology and policy support are paving the way for broader adoption of decentralized systems. Smart [water management tools like sensors](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/346335079_Smart_Water_Technology_for_Efficient_Water_Resource_Management_A_Review) and data analytics are improving system efficiency and reliability. Digital tools such as Internet of Things (IoT) devices and cloud-based analytics are revolutionizing how these systems operate and are maintained.

Smart sensors monitor water quality parameters like pH, turbidity and contaminant levels in real time, enabling rapid response to any issues. IoT-enabled devices allow for remote system control and diagnostics, reducing the need for on-site personnel while improving efficiency.

Cloud-based platforms aggregate data from multiple locations, offering actionable insights that help optimize treatment processes and predict maintenance needs. These technologies also make it easier to comply with water safety regulations.

Automated data logging simplifies reporting requirements, while predictive analytics helps identify potential system failures before they occur, reducing downtime and extending equipment life span.

By integrating data and digital tools, decentralized water systems are more efficient and resilient, ensuring they can continue to provide reliable access to clean water under varying conditions.

Additionally, government incentives and public-private partnerships are helping to bridge funding gaps. For example, the World Bank has [approved funding to promote decentralized water](https://smartwatermagazine.com/news/world-bank/world-bank-approves-us80-million-decentralized-water-and-sanitation-rural-haiti) solutions in countries like Haiti, combining technical assistance with financial support to build local capacity.

**A Path Forward**

Decentralized water treatment systems are reshaping how communities manage their water resources. By focusing on localized solutions, these systems empower communities to take control of their water quality and ensure access to clean, safe drinking water.

Policymakers, engineers and community leaders have a unique opportunity to scale these systems, addressing global water challenges while promoting sustainability and resilience. As technology advances and costs decrease, decentralized water treatment is poised to become a cornerstone of modern water management strategies.

By investing in and supporting these innovative systems, society can move closer to achieving universal access to clean water — a fundamental human right and a critical component of sustainable development.

**Ellie Gabel** is a freelance writer living in Raleigh, North Carolina, United States. She has covered environmental issues and advancements in green technology for a variety of publications. She also works as an associate editor for *Revolutionized*.

The views expressed in this article belong to the individual authors and do not represent the views of the Global Water Forum, the UNESCO Chair in Water Economics and Transboundary Water Governance, UNESCO, the Australian National University, World Bank, Oxford University, or any of the institutions to which the authors are associated. Please see the Global Water Forum terms and conditions [here](https://globalwaterforum.org/about/).

![](https://www.globalwaterforum.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Constructed_wetland_5268650754-800x600.jpg)

### Interested in Infrastructure?

Get Infrastructure articles, news and videos right in your inbox! Sign up now.

As communities grow and infrastructure ages, decentralized wastewater treatment systems are gaining attention as a flexible and cost-effective alternative to traditional plants. These local solutions provide tailored treatment for specific areas, reducing strain on larger systems.

Decentralized systems are particularly valuable in rural or rapidly developing areas where extending centralized infrastructure is costly. They also offer resiliency in the face of extreme weather events, as localized systems are less likely to experience widespread failures.

Technological advancements are making these systems more efficient, environmentally friendly, and easier to maintain. Innovations in monitoring and automation ensure compliance and reliability, giving developers the confidence to embrace this approach.

With growing interest in sustainable and scalable infrastructure, decentralized treatment represents a significant shift toward smarter water management. As more communities explore this option, the industry must be ready to meet the demand with innovative solutions.

What’s your take on decentralized wastewater treatment? Share your experiences!

# Revitalizing Rural America: The Rise of Decentralized Wastewater Solutions for Sustainable Living

**Written by:** [Nathan Smith](https://www.linkedin.com/in/nathancwsmith/), SVP, Marketing & Lifecycle Leader – Newterra

In the landscape of American infrastructure, rural communities often find themselves on the periphery, grappling with outdated or non-existent wastewater systems. The shift toward centralized treatment facilities in the past half-century, while beneficial for urban centers, has frequently overlooked these less populated areas, leaving them with a legacy of environmental and economic challenges. Yet, there is a growing recognition of the potential in [decentralized wastewater systems](https://www.newterra.com/technology/decentralized-modular-potable-water-systems/ "Decentralized Modular Potable Water Systems"), which promise a more sustainable and self-reliant future for these rural settings.

Decentralized systems, with their adaptability and smaller scale, offer a pragmatic solution that aligns with the realities of rural life. They represent more than just an alternative technical approach; they embody a shift towards empowering communities to manage their own resources more effectively. This move not only addresses the immediate need for reliable wastewater treatment but also echoes a broader theme of sustainable development, where environmental health and community well-being go hand in hand.

As we explore the journey of rural areas from infrastructural neglect to a future of autonomy and resilience, it becomes clear that decentralized wastewater treatment is not just a utility upgrade. It’s a step towards rectifying historical oversights and paving the way for a more balanced and equitable infrastructure development across the nation.

## **The Plight of Rural Infrastructure and Historical Context of Wastewater Treatment**

The pivot to centralized wastewater treatment in the United States marks a significant chapter in the nation’s environmental policy, propelled by the [Clean Water Act of 1972](https://www.epa.gov/laws-regulations/summary-clean-water-act#:~:text=33%20U.S.C.%20%C2%A71251%20et,quality%20standards%20for%20surface%20waters.). This legislative milestone, aimed at reducing pollution and improving water quality, spurred the construction of large-scale treatment plants, centralizing waste management in urban centers. While these advancements heralded a new era of environmental responsibility, they inadvertently deepened the infrastructural divide between urban and rural America.

Rural areas, with their dispersed populations and limited financial resources, found themselves at a disadvantage. The economies of scale that made centralized systems viable in cities did not translate well to the countryside, where the cost and complexity of extending large sewer networks are prohibitively high. As a result, many rural communities continued to rely on outdated or inadequate systems, such as septic tanks and cesspools, which were often ill-equipped to meet the growing environmental and public health standards.

The implications of this centralization trend extended beyond mere infrastructure disparities. In many rural locales, the lack of modern wastewater treatment facilities has not only perpetuated environmental challenges but also stunted economic growth. Over 2 million Americans, predominantly in rural areas, live without proper wastewater infrastructure, a critical public health concern. This issue is prevalent in rural areas where poverty is often more common, and residents might rely on ineffective on-site treatment systems or practices like straight piping, which discharges untreated wastewater directly into the environment.

Poor wastewater management leads to water contamination, affecting agriculture, tourism, and overall quality of life. Additionally, the ongoing environmental neglect threatens the delicate balance of rural ecosystems, undermining long-term sustainability and resilience.

Thus, the historical shift towards centralized wastewater treatment, while successful in urban contexts, has underscored the need for a more inclusive approach to infrastructure planning. For rural America, the legacy of centralization is a complex web of challenges, weaving together issues of health, economy, and environmental stewardship. Recognizing these challenges is the first step in rethinking rural wastewater management, setting the stage for decentralized systems as a pathway to rectification and renewal.

## **Decentralized Wastewater Treatment: A Path to Autonomy**

Decentralized wastewater treatment marks a paradigm shift, steering away from the uniform approach of centralization to embrace systems tailored to the distinct needs of rural landscapes. This evolution is characterized by localizing waste treatment, eliminating the extensive infrastructure typically associated with urban sewer networks, thus offering an economically and environmentally sound alternative for sparsely populated regions.

Central to this decentralized approach is the principle of local management and maintenance, aligning perfectly with the rural lifestyle. This method alleviates the logistical and financial strains associated with large-scale infrastructure projects, granting communities the power to control their environmental health. The progress in this field has been significant, with technologies like [Membrane BioReactors](https://www.newterra.com/technologies/membrane-bioreactor-plants/) (MBRs) leading the charge. These advanced systems are renowned for their high-quality treatment capabilities within a compact design, making them exceptionally suited for rural areas where space and resources are limited.

MBRs stand out as a substantial improvement on traditional septic systems, offering a level of treatment that produces effluent clean enough for reuse in agricultural irrigation or for replenishing local water bodies. The average cost of installing a septic system for a single household ranges from $10,000 to $30,000, underscoring the financial burden on rural households. Solutions, like MBRs, offer the benefits of community-based solutions without placing impossible burdens on individual families. This not only demonstrates the economic and technical superiority of these systems but also underscores their role in promoting sustainable water management practices within rural communities.

The benefits of decentralized wastewater systems extend beyond the practicalities of their installation and operation. They empower rural communities with the autonomy to manage their infrastructure, fostering enhanced stewardship of local water resources. This independence strengthens the bond between residents and their environment, encouraging practices that support sustainability and conservation. By adapting these advanced solutions to the specific needs and capabilities of each community, decentralized treatment effectively meets the unique challenges of rural areas, ensuring compliance with environmental and public health standards without compromising economic feasibility.

In essence, decentralized wastewater treatment directly supports the autonomy  and long term survival of rural America, offering a sustainable pathway for infrastructure management that respects rural intricacies. It addresses the enduring challenges of environmental stewardship and community well-being, envisioning a future where rural areas can flourish with independence and sustainability.

## **Empowerment Through Independence and Autonomy**

Empowerment in rural areas is intrinsically linked to the autonomy afforded by advanced decentralized wastewater systems. This independence in managing essential services like wastewater treatment catalyzes improved community decision-making and public health outcomes. When local stakeholders take the reins, they tailor solutions to their specific environmental and socioeconomic contexts, resulting in more effective and responsive management practices.

This localized approach to wastewater management does more than solve technical challenges; it fosters a sense of ownership and responsibility among community members. The direct involvement in the operational aspects of these systems leads to a better understanding of environmental stewardship, heightening collective awareness and action towards sustainable practices. As residents witness the tangible benefits of improved wastewater management — such as cleaner waterways, reduced disease prevalence, and enhanced environmental quality — their commitment to maintaining these systems intensifies.

Moreover, the autonomy associated with decentralized wastewater treatment has broader implications for community well-being and resilience. It equips rural communities with the tools and knowledge to respond proactively to environmental challenges, reducing dependency on external entities for infrastructural support. This self-sufficiency not only strengthens the social fabric of these communities but also enhances their capacity to withstand and adapt to ecological and economic fluctuations.

In essence, the move towards decentralized systems and the resultant autonomy it brings encapsulates a shift towards more empowered, informed, and resilient rural communities. This empowerment is a testament to the interconnectedness of local governance, environmental health, and community well-being, painting a future where rural areas are not just surviving but thriving, anchored by sustainable and autonomous infrastructure solutions.

## **Economic and Social Benefits**

The economic and social benefits of decentralized wastewater treatment systems are multi-faceted, particularly in rural areas where they provide vital sanitation services. [A study published in Scientific Reports](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-022-18852-y) highlighted that decentralized systems have significantly lower electricity consumption compared to centralized systems, with decentralized scenarios using only 0.363 kWh/m³ treated wastewater compared to the centralized treatment’s 0.873 kWh/m³. This efficiency translates into direct cost savings and reduced environmental impact, underscoring the economic advantage of decentralized approaches in areas where extending centralized infrastructure is not viable.

Indirect economic benefits of decentralized systems include local economic stimulation through job creation in system installation, maintenance, and operation. Moreover, improved sanitation facilities contribute to enhanced public health, reducing medical costs and increasing productivity by preventing waterborne diseases prevalent in areas with inadequate wastewater management.

Socially, the implementation of decentralized wastewater treatment improves the quality of life for rural residents. Access to reliable sanitation facilities enhances community well-being, supports sustainable development, and promotes environmental stewardship. Improved water quality from effective wastewater treatment not only benefits human health but also supports local agriculture and ecosystems, contributing to the overall resilience and sustainability of rural communities.

In summary, decentralized wastewater treatment systems offer significant economic and social benefits, particularly in rural areas. They provide a cost-effective and efficient solution to sanitation needs, stimulate local economies, improve public health, and enhance the quality of life, making them a valuable investment in the sustainable development of rural regions.

## **Future Prospects: Enhancing Rural Viability Through Investment, Awareness, and Policy**

The future of enhancing rural viability through decentralized wastewater treatment in the United States intertwines increased investment, awareness, and robust policy frameworks. The EPA and USDA’s “[Closing America’s Wastewater Access Gap Community Initiative](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2022-08/Fact%20Sheet%20Closing%20the%20Wastewater%20Access%20Gap%20FINAL.pdf)” targets underserved communities, aiming to provide sanitation services in rural areas. This initiative reflects the national effort to bridge the infrastructure gap in these communities.

At the state level, states like Texas and Florida have taken proactive approaches in transitioning from septic to sewer systems, showcasing significant legislative and financial commitments to improve water infrastructure and manage water resources sustainably.

In Texas, the legislative action through [Senate Bill 28](https://legiscan.com/TX/bill/SB28/2023) allocated $1 billion for water infrastructure upgrades, including septic-to-sewer projects, aiming to address water scarcity and support a growing population. This initiative is part of a larger strategy to utilize state surplus funds to match federal investments, thereby expanding resources for comprehensive water management solutions.

Florida’s focused efforts in regions like Lee County, where the state Wastewater Grant Program funded septic-to-sewer conversions, highlight the targeted approach to reduce nutrient pollution and improve waterway health. These projects, part of a $240 million initiative, demonstrate the state’s commitment to environmental sustainability and public health.

These state-level actions, supported by federal programs like the [Clean Water State Revolving Fund](https://www.epa.gov/cwsrf), provide low-interest financing for water quality projects, enabling the implementation of advanced decentralized systems like Membrane BioReactors (MBRs). These systems are not only cost-effective and environmentally sustainable but also crucial for rural areas where traditional centralized treatment is unfeasible.

Raising awareness and educating communities on the benefits and operations of decentralized systems is vital. The EPA’s [Decentralized Wastewater Management MOU Partnership](https://www.epa.gov/septic/epas-decentralized-wastewater-partnership) emphasizes improving decentralized system management through collaboration, public education, and information exchange, underscoring the importance of community engagement in sustainable wastewater management.

Policy advancements are essential to encourage the adoption of decentralized systems. Legislative support at both state and federal levels is needed to integrate these systems into rural infrastructure planning effectively. Policies that promote the use of advanced decentralized systems, streamline approval processes, and provide clear implementation guidelines are crucial for enhancing their uptake.

In conclusion, enhancing rural viability through decentralized wastewater treatment hinges on strategic investment, comprehensive awareness campaigns, and supportive policy frameworks. By leveraging examples from states like Texas and Florida and focusing on these key areas, stakeholders can ensure that rural communities have access to sustainable, efficient, and cost-effective wastewater management solutions.

## **Conclusion**

As we contemplate the journey of decentralized wastewater treatment, its transformative potential for rural communities becomes undeniably clear. This approach not only champions the ethos of independence and autonomy but also significantly enhances the quality of life through sustainable infrastructure development. Decentralized systems, tailored to the specific needs and constraints of rural areas, embody a future where communities can manage their resources efficiently, fostering environmental stewardship and resilience.

We invite others to delve deeper into this critical discourse. Share your insights, experiences, and questions through our web forms or join the conversation on our LinkedIn platform. Your perspectives enrich the dialogue, helping to shape a more nuanced understanding of decentralized wastewater treatment’s role in rural sustainability.

For those seeking to explore further, [Newterra.com](https://www.newterra.com) offers a wealth of resources. [Downloadable materials](https://www.newterra.com/resources/) are available to provide additional value, supporting an informed and engaged approach to wastewater management in rural settings.

In closing, the path to a sustainable and empowered rural future is paved with knowledge, collaboration, and innovative solutions like decentralized wastewater treatment. Together, we can help shape a more resilient and autonomous rural landscape.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Decentralized systems provide on-site wastewater treatment, addressing local needs directly while minimizing the infrastructure costs associated with centralized facilities.
These systems can be easily scaled up or down based on demand, allowing communities to adapt to changing populations and wastewater treatment requirements.
Decentralized wastewater treatment can incorporate renewable energy sources, such as solar or wind, enhancing sustainability and reducing operational costs for communities.
